status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: IoT wearable device for elderly fall detection with real-time caregiver alerts, GPS tracking, and automatic emergency dispatch. Must comply with FDA Class II and IEC 62304 for medical device software.

plan.description: Conduct ISO 14971 risk management: identify hazards (missed fall, false alarm, GPS failure, alert delay), estimate severity/probability for each, define risk controls, and produce Risk Management File (RMF) and Hazard Analysis Report.

payload.intended_use: Continuous wearable fall detection for elderly users with automated emergency alerting
hazards: Undetected fall leading to delayed emergency response
False positive alarm causing unnecessary emergency dispatch

acceptance_criteria: All identified hazards have severity and probability ratings (S1–S5, P1–P5)
Residual risk for every hazard is ALARP and within FDA/ISO 14971 acceptability thresholds
Risk Management Plan is signed and version-controlled
Hazard-to-requirement traceability is established for each risk control
ISO 14971 risk management file complete
Residual risk acceptable

plan.description: Establish the Design History File (DHF) skeleton per FDA 21 CFR Part 820.30 and ISO 13485. Define IEC 62304 software lifecycle documentation structure (Software Development Plan, Software Requirements Spec, Software Architecture Doc, V&V protocols). Map all applicable standards and create traceability matrix shell.
acceptance_criteria: DHF structure covers all 21 CFR 820.30 required sections
IEC 62304 Software Development Plan is drafted with lifecycle phases, roles, and deliverables
Traceability matrix links user needs → design inputs → design outputs → V&V
Document control numbering scheme is defined and applied to all artifacts
Software safety class justification (Class B) is documented with rationale
DHF (Design History File) structure created
V&V protocol drafted

plan.description: Define system architecture and product requirements: wearable hardware specs (MCU, IMU, GPS, cellular/BLE), cloud backend topology, caregiver mobile app scope, fall detection algorithm approach, emergency dispatch integration (911 API or PSAP gateway), and user personas (elderly wearer, caregiver, emergency responder).

system_components: Wearable device (MCU + 6-axis IMU + GPS + LTE-M/NB-IoT + BLE + battery)
Cloud backend (alert engine, GPS tracker, emergency dispatch, device management)
Emergency dispatch integration (PSAP or third-party SOS relay)
key_requirements: Fall detection latency < 3 seconds from event
Alert delivery to caregiver < 10 seconds from detection
False positive rate < 2% during normal ADL

acceptance_criteria: System architecture diagram approved showing all components and data flows
Numbered design input requirements (≥50) traceable to user needs
Performance requirements have measurable acceptance criteria
Use cases for fall detection, caregiver alert, and emergency dispatch are documented
Out-of-scope items explicitly listed

plan.description: Produce threat model (STRIDE) for device-to-cloud and cloud-to-caregiver communication paths. Define security architecture: TLS 1.3 for all transport, AES-256 for data at rest, device attestation, OTA firmware signing, HIPAA-aligned PHI handling. Generate SBOM for all software components.

security_controls: Secure element for device identity (X.509 cert)
OAuth 2.0 + PKCE for caregiver app auth
API rate limiting on alert endpoints
HIPAA Business Associate Agreement with cloud provider

acceptance_criteria: STRIDE analysis completed for all 5 system interfaces
Every HIGH and CRITICAL threat has a documented mitigation control
SBOM lists all third-party libraries with CVE scan results (zero known critical CVEs)
Penetration test plan is drafted for pre-market validation phase
HIPAA PHI data flows are identified and encryption controls documented
Device identity provisioned
Firmware signing enabled

plan.description: Design caregiver mobile app UX and wearable device interaction model. Wireframe alert notification flow, location map view, device status dashboard, emergency dispatch confirmation screen, and onboarding flow. Conduct accessibility audit for elderly user interaction with device (single-button SOS, haptic feedback, LED indicators).

personas: Elderly wearer (limited tech proficiency, possible cognitive decline)
screens: Caregiver app: Fall alert push notification + accept/dismiss
Caregiver app: Real-time GPS map with wearer location
Caregiver app: Device battery and connectivity status
Caregiver app: Alert history and fall event log
Caregiver app: Emergency dispatch confirmation dialog
Caregiver app: Wearer profile and contact setup
Device: Single SOS button interaction + haptic/LED feedback spec

acceptance_criteria: All screens wireframed and reviewed by representative caregivers
Alert-to-acknowledge flow completes in ≤3 taps
Device interaction spec defines SOS button hold duration, LED patterns, and haptic sequences
Accessibility audit report shows WCAG 2.1 AA compliance
Usability test with 5 participants shows task success rate ≥90% for critical flows

plan.description: Capture hardware schematic: MCU (Nordic nRF9161 with LTE-M/NB-IoT + DECT NR+), 6-axis IMU (LSM6DSO32X), GPS module (u-blox M10), BLE 5.3, Li-Po battery with fuel gauge (MAX17048), USB-C charging, secure element (ATECC608B). Generate BOM and apply DRC rules for medical-grade PCB (IPC Class 3).

payload.imu: STMicro LSM6DSO32X (±32g accelerometer, gyroscope, machine learning core)
payload.secure_element: Microchip ATECC608B (TLS auth, firmware signature verification)

acceptance_criteria: Schematic passes ERC with zero errors
BOM includes manufacturer part numbers and approved alternates for all components
DRC passes for IPC Class 3 spacing rules
Power budget analysis shows ≥72h battery life at nominal operation
IMU interrupt line routed to MCU wake pin for low-power fall detection

plan.description: Design cloud data model: time-series sensor data (accelerometer events, GPS positions), fall events, alert records, device registry, caregiver-wearer relationships, and audit trail. Define retention policies, HIPAA-compliant de-identification strategy, and backup/recovery RPO/RTO targets.

payload.database_stack: PostgreSQL (relational) + TimescaleDB (time-series sensor data) + Redis (alert fan-out pub/sub)
core_tables: fall_events (event_id, device_id, timestamp, confidence_score, location_lat, location_lng, alert_status)
gps_positions (device_id, timestamp, lat, lng, accuracy_m, source) — TimescaleDB hypertable
alerts (alert_id, fall_event_id, caregiver_id, sent_at, acknowledged_at, escalated_to_911)
hipaa_controls: PHI fields encrypted at column level (pgcrypto AES-256)
Retention: sensor data 90 days, fall events 7 years

acceptance_criteria: ER diagram reviewed and approved by engineering lead
All migrations run cleanly on PostgreSQL 15+ with TimescaleDB extension
PHI columns encrypted and decryption requires authenticated service identity
Audit log table has insert-only policy (no UPDATE/DELETE permissions on app role)
RTO ≤ 4h, RPO ≤ 1h documented and tested with restore drill

plan.description: Design OpenAPI 3.1 specification for all system interfaces: device-to-cloud MQTT/HTTPS protocol (telemetry ingestion, OTA commands), cloud REST API (caregiver app, device management), and webhook contract for emergency dispatch integration. Define versioning strategy and error codes.

payload.device_protocol: MQTT over TLS 1.3 (AWS IoT Core or Azure IoT Hub) for telemetry; HTTPS for OTA
payload.emergency_dispatch_webhook: POST to third-party PSAP relay (RapidSOS API or equivalent)

acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec validates against OpenAPI 3.1 schema with zero errors
All endpoints have request/response schemas, error responses (400/401/403/404/500), and examples
MQTT topic hierarchy documented with QoS levels and retention flags
Emergency dispatch webhook contract tested against RapidSOS sandbox
API versioning strategy documented (URI versioning v1/v2)

plan.description: Implement fall detection firmware on nRF9161 using Zephyr RTOS. IMU HAL driver (LSM6DSO32X SPI), fall detection algorithm (threshold-based + ML Core pattern recognition), RTOS tasks for sensor polling, fall event state machine (free-fall → impact → post-fall posture), and SOS button ISR handler. Implements IEC 62304 Class B software unit requirements.

payload.toolchain: nRF Connect SDK + ARM GNU Toolchain
payload.imu_driver: SPI HAL driver for LSM6DSO32X with DMA transfer

fall_algorithm.ml_assist: LSM6DSO32X Machine Learning Core with pre-loaded fall decision tree

rtos_tasks: imu_task: 200Hz IMU polling, circular buffer, fall state machine
gps_task: 1Hz position fix, power-managed (duty cycle when stationary)
comm_task: MQTT publish telemetry + fall events via LTE-M
ble_task: BLE pairing/bonding for app-direct proximity alert
power_task: Battery monitoring, low-battery alert, sleep orchestration
ota_task: FOTA update download + signature verify + apply
payload.sos_button: GPIO interrupt → immediate alert bypass (skip 30s wait window)

acceptance_criteria: IMU driver passes loopback self-test on boot with zero SPI errors
Fall state machine correctly classifies ≥95% of simulated fall sequences in bench test
False positive rate < 2% on 60-minute ADL simulation (walking, sitting, arm raises)
Fall-to-MQTT-publish latency < 2 seconds measured on logic analyzer
SOS button ISR triggers within 50ms of press (measured)
Firmware image signed with ECDSA P-256 private key and signature verified on boot
All firmware modules have IEC 62304 software unit documentation (inputs, outputs, rationale)
IEC 62304 software class documented
SOUP components listed
OTA update mechanism implemented
Watchdog timer configured

plan.description: Implement GPS and cellular communication firmware: u-blox M10 UART driver, NMEA parsing, geofencing zone logic, LTE-M/NB-IoT modem management (nRF9161 AT commands), MQTT client (MQTT-SN over LTE-M), TLS 1.3 stack configuration, and OTA firmware update (FOTA) manager with rollback.

payload.gps_driver: UART HAL for u-blox M10, NMEA 0183 GPRMC/GPGGA parser
payload.cellular_modem: nRF9161 modem firmware + AT command abstraction layer
payload.mqtt_client: Eclipse Paho MQTT C client, QoS 1 for fall events, QoS 0 for telemetry
payload.tls_config: mbedTLS 3.x, TLS 1.3 only, mTLS with device X.509 cert from ATECC608B
payload.fota: nRF Cloud FOTA service integration, download resume on disconnect, ECDSA signature verify before apply, automatic rollback on 3 consecutive boot failures
payload.offline_buffer: Circular flash buffer (8KB) to store events during connectivity loss, flush on reconnect

acceptance_criteria: GPS first fix < 30s cold start, < 5s hot start in open sky
GPS position accuracy verified ≤10m with u-blox EVK reference comparison
MQTT connection established within 15s of LTE-M registration
TLS 1.3 handshake verified with Wireshark — no downgrade to TLS 1.2
FOTA: corrupted image rejected (checksum fail) and previous firmware boots successfully
Offline buffer stores ≥100 events and flushes in order on reconnect (verified in network outage test)
IEC 62304 software class documented
SOUP components listed
OTA update mechanism implemented
Watchdog timer configured

plan.description: Build cloud alert engine and emergency dispatch service: MQTT ingestion from AWS IoT Core, fall event processor (deduplication, confidence filtering), caregiver notification fan-out (push notification + SMS + in-app), 30-second no-response escalation timer, and RapidSOS API integration for automatic 911 dispatch with GPS location payload.

payload.stack: Python 3.11 + FastAPI + Celery (async tasks) + Redis (pub/sub + rate limiting)
payload.cloud: AWS (IoT Core, ECS Fargate, RDS PostgreSQL, ElastiCache Redis, SNS, SES)
alert_pipeline: 1. MQTT message received by IoT Core rule → SQS queue
2. Alert processor validates device, filters confidence < 0.85
3. Deduplication: suppress duplicate fall events within 60s window
4. Fan-out: FCM push + Twilio SMS + WebSocket to app
5. Start 30s Celery countdown task for escalation
6. If acknowledged: cancel countdown, log resolution
7. If not acknowledged: POST to RapidSOS /emergency with GPS + wearer profile
payload.rapidsos_integration: RapidSOS NG911 API: POST /incidents with location (lat/lng/accuracy), wearer name, medical notes, device battery level

acceptance_criteria: Fall event to caregiver push notification delivered in < 5 seconds (measured end-to-end)
Deduplication correctly suppresses duplicate events within 60s window (unit tested)
30-second escalation timer fires within ±1 second of deadline
RapidSOS API call succeeds with correct GPS payload in integration test against sandbox
Dead-letter queue captures and retries failed notifications with exponential backoff
Alert engine handles 1000 concurrent fall events without degradation (load test)

plan.description: Build GPS tracking service and device management API: real-time location ingestion pipeline, caregiver map API (WebSocket live updates + REST history), geofence management (define safe zones, alert on exit), device registry CRUD, device health monitoring (battery, last-seen, connectivity), and OTA update orchestration.

payload.location_pipeline: MQTT → IoT Core → Kinesis Data Streams → TimescaleDB (GPS hypertable)
payload.websocket: FastAPI WebSocket endpoint /ws/wearers/{id}/location — pushes GPS updates every 5s to connected caregivers
payload.geofence: PostGIS ST_Within() check on each GPS fix against wearer's configured safe zones; alert trigger on breach
device_management: GET/POST/PATCH /devices — CRUD with firmware version tracking
GET /devices/fleet — all devices for organization with health summary
payload.health_monitoring: Celery Beat task every 60s: flag devices with no heartbeat >5min as OFFLINE, alert caregivers

acceptance_criteria: GPS position stored in TimescaleDB within 2 seconds of device MQTT publish
WebSocket live location updates delivered to caregiver app within 1 second of ingestion
Geofence breach detected and alert triggered within 10 seconds of GPS fix crossing boundary
OTA update job successfully pushed to device and FOTA completion confirmed via device telemetry
Device offline detection alerts caregiver within 6 minutes of device going offline

payload.map: MapLibre React Native — real-time wearer location pin with accuracy radius

screens: AlertScreen: Full-screen fall alert with wearer name, location, time, Call / Dismiss / Dispatch 911 actions
MapScreen: Live GPS map, location history trail (last 24h), geofence zone overlay
DashboardScreen: Battery %, connectivity, last activity, today's alert count

acceptance_criteria: Fall alert push notification received and AlertScreen opens within 3 seconds on both iOS 17 and Android 14
Map renders wearer GPS location with ≤2s delay from WebSocket update

App passes Accessibility audit (WCAG 2.1 AA) on both platforms

plan.description: Develop hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test suite for fall detection firmware: automated fall simulation rig (IMU signal injection via JTAG/SWD), test cases for all fall scenarios, firmware unit tests (Zephyr Test Framework), and integration test harness validating end-to-end flow from IMU trigger to MQTT publish.

payload.hil_rig: Nordic PPK2 (power measurement) + J-Link + custom IMU signal injector (STM32 replaying recorded fall data)
test_datasets: Simfall dataset: 200 fall events (forward, backward, sideways, stair fall)
ADL dataset: 500 activities of daily living (walking, running, sitting, arm raise, gym exercise)
Edge cases: near-falls, stumbles, rapid directional changes
unit_tests: test_imu_driver: SPI read/write, FIFO overflow handling, self-test pass
test_fall_state_machine: All 8 state transitions with correct timing
test_sos_button: ISR latency, debounce, long-press vs short-press
test_offline_buffer: Circular buffer wraparound, flush order guarantee
test_fota_signature: Valid sig accepts, invalid sig rejects (5 tampered images)
integration_tests: test_fall_to_mqtt: IMU inject → state machine → MQTT publish measured < 2s
test_network_loss_recovery: Inject fall during LTE outage → buffer → reconnect → flush
test_battery_low_alert: Discharge to 15% → alert MQTT → caregiver notified

acceptance_criteria: Unit test coverage ≥ 80% of firmware source lines (measured with gcov)
Fall detection sensitivity ≥ 95% on 200-event simfall dataset
ADL false positive rate < 2% on 500-event ADL dataset
All 7 unit test suites pass with zero failures on clean build
HIL integration test for fall-to-MQTT passes 50 consecutive runs without failure

plan.description: Develop system-level HIL tests: full device power cycle, LTE-M network registration, GPS acquisition, fall detection → cloud alert → caregiver notification end-to-end flow. Stress test battery life measurement (72h continuous with representative duty cycle). Environmental testing spec (IP67 ingress, operating temperature -10°C to 50°C).

e2e_test_scenarios: Cold boot → LTE-M registration → GPS fix → fall inject → MQTT publish → cloud alert → FCM push → caregiver app alert display
Network loss recovery: fall inject during LTE outage → local buffer → reconnect → delayed alert < 30s
SOS button override: press during normal operation → immediate alert bypass 30s timer
Caregiver no-response escalation: fall inject → no acknowledge within 30s → RapidSOS API call
OTA update: push FOTA → device downloads → verifies → applies → reboots → confirms new version to cloud
payload.battery_test: Automated 72h runtime test with Nordic PPK2 logging current at 1Hz; expected average current < 1.5mA
payload.environmental_spec: IP67 ingress (30min at 1m depth), -10°C to 50°C operational, 10 x 1m drop onto concrete (IEC 60068-2-31)

acceptance_criteria: E2E fall alert flow completes successfully in 100 consecutive test runs
72h battery test shows device operational for ≥72h at rated duty cycle
IP67 test: device fully functional after 30-min submersion at 1m
Drop test: device operational and fall detection functional after 10 x 1m drops

plan.description: Build CI/CD pipelines: firmware build pipeline (GitHub Actions + nRF Connect SDK Docker container, automated HIL test trigger on PR), cloud backend pipeline (Python lint + pytest + Docker build + ECS deploy with blue-green), mobile app pipeline (Expo EAS Build + Detox tests + TestFlight/Play Console deploy). Add infrastructure-as-code (Terraform) for all AWS resources.

payload.firmware_pipeline: GitHub Actions: nRF Connect SDK build → binary artifact → HIL test trigger (self-hosted runner with J-Link) → FOTA staging push on main merge
payload.backend_pipeline: GitHub Actions: ruff lint → pytest → Docker build → ECR push → ECS Fargate blue-green deploy → smoke test

plan.description: Execute penetration test plan against all system surfaces: firmware binary analysis (Binwalk, Ghidra), cloud API fuzzing (OWASP ZAP), mobile app static analysis (MobSF), MQTT broker attack surface (unauthorized publish/subscribe), TLS downgrade attempts, and OTA spoofing simulation. Remediate all HIGH/CRITICAL findings.

payload.firmware_analysis: Binwalk entropy analysis for embedded secrets; Ghidra static analysis for unsafe functions (strcpy, sprintf); verify secure boot chain
payload.api_pentest: OWASP ZAP automated scan + manual testing: auth bypass, IDOR on wearer/device IDs, mass assignment, rate limiting bypass
payload.mobile_pentest: MobSF static: hardcoded secrets, insecure storage, exported Activities; dynamic: traffic interception, certificate pinning bypass attempt
payload.mqtt_attack: Unauthorized MQTT subscribe to other device topics; unauthorized publish to fall-event topic; broker ACL bypass
payload.ota_attack: Attempt OTA with unsigned image, downgrade attack (old firmware), replay attack with captured FOTA session

acceptance_criteria: Zero CRITICAL vulnerabilities in final penetration test report
Zero HIGH vulnerabilities unmitigated (all either fixed or accepted with compensating controls)
Firmware binary contains no hardcoded credentials (verified with trufflehog scan)
Certificate pinning bypass attempt fails on both iOS and Android
MQTT ACL prevents cross-device topic access (verified with unauthorized subscribe test)
OTA with unsigned or downgrade image rejected by device (verified in 10 attack scenarios)
Penetration test report stored in DHF as cybersecurity validation evidence
Device identity provisioned
Firmware signing enabled

metrics: SOS button reachable within 3s by participant

payload.field_trial: 30-day trial with 10 participants: log all alerts, false positives, battery performance, device uptime

acceptance_criteria: Clinical simulation: sensitivity ≥ 95%, false positive rate ≤ 2% per 4h (primary validation endpoints)
Caregiver UAT: SUS score ≥ 80, task completion ≥ 95%

plan.description: Develop training materials: clinical staff onboarding (device setup, caregiver app admin, alert response protocol), caregiver app onboarding video series (5 x 3-minute videos), and regulatory training record for IEC 62304 compliance (all engineering staff trained on software lifecycle procedures).

clinical_training: Device provisioning and pairing procedure (step-by-step with screenshots)

acceptance_criteria: Clinical training materials reviewed and approved by clinical operations manager
Caregiver video series tested with 5 representative caregivers (all complete setup unaided after viewing)
IEC 62304 training: 100% of engineering staff complete training with ≥80% quiz score
Training records stored in QMS with employee signatures and completion dates
Training materials version-controlled and updated with each major software release

flaws: IEC 62304 Software Safety Class misclassification: Class B ('injury possible') is almost certainly wrong. An undetected fall causing an elderly person to lie unresponsive for hours can be fatal — that is Class C ('death possible'). Class B requires only software unit testing; Class C requires full integration and system testing with traceability to every software item. If FDA reviewers disagree with your Class B justification, the entire IEC 62304 software documentation package must be redone. This is submission-killing if wrong.

510(k) predicate assumption is unvalidated and deferred too late. The predicate device search is listed as an acceptance criterion in Step 18 — after 17 steps of design work. If no adequate predicate exists under MVO, or if FDA classifies automatic emergency dispatch as a novel feature requiring De Novo, the entire regulatory strategy collapses. Predicate identification must happen in Step 2, not Step 18.

No IRB/Ethics Board approval step. Step 22 involves controlled fall simulations with elderly participants on padded mats and a 30-day field trial. This requires IRB approval (45 CFR Part 46 / 21 CFR Part 50) before any human subject study begins. Proceeding without IRB approval invalidates the clinical data for FDA submission and exposes the organization to serious legal liability.

Indoor GPS is the primary failure mode and it's unaddressed. Falls predominantly happen indoors — bathroom, bedroom, kitchen. u-blox M10 GPS will not achieve ≤10m accuracy indoors; it often cannot acquire a fix at all. There is no fallback positioning strategy (WiFi positioning, BLE beacon triangulation, cell tower triangulation, last-known-outdoors position). The '≤10 meters' GPS spec will fail the majority of real-world use cases.

400mAh battery vs. 72h runtime with LTE-M + GPS is not credible without a detailed power budget. LTE-M active transmit current on nRF9161 is 220-490mA peak; GPS acquisition is 15-20mA; Zephyr RTOS active MCU is 5-15mA. Even with aggressive duty cycling (eDRX, PSM), 400mAh / 72h = 5.5mA average budget is extremely tight. Step 6 acceptance criteria require a power budget analysis, but the 400mAh figure is already specified as a fixed constraint in the BOM — if the power budget fails, the hardware must be redesigned. This should be validated before committing to the form factor.

30-second escalation timer for automatic 911 dispatch has no legal framework. Automatic emergency dispatch is regulated differently in each US state. False-positive 911 calls can result in liability under FCC rules and state statutes. No legal review step exists in this plan. RapidSOS NG911 API access requires a signed agreement with RapidSOS, carrier integration agreements, and may not have PSAP coverage in all geographies. The plan treats this as a pure engineering problem.

Clinical validation sample size is inadequate for FDA evidence. 30 participants (10 for field trial) is far below what FDA expects for a Class II device with a primary safety endpoint. Predicate devices in the fall detection space have used 100-300 participants for pivotal validation. The 'controlled fall simulation on padded mats' does not replicate real fall biomechanics. FDA will likely require clinical data from spontaneous or minimally-assisted falls. This validation plan will not support a 510(k) submission.

Fall detection algorithm training data provenance and demographic coverage are unspecified. The ML Core decision tree is 'pre-loaded' but there is no specification of what dataset it was trained on, whether it covers the target demographic (elderly, 65+, varying BMIs, mobility aids like walkers/canes), or whether the training data is independent from the validation data. This is a SOUP qualification gap under IEC 62304 and a performance validation gap for FDA.

No Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) Plan. FDA 21 CFR Part 822 requires a post-market surveillance plan for Class II devices. ISO 13485 requires post-market monitoring. MDR (Medical Device Reporting) procedures under 21 CFR Part 803 for adverse event reporting are completely absent. These are not optional and must exist before commercialization.

No FCC / RF Certification step. The nRF9161 LTE-M radio and BLE 5.3 require FCC Part 15/22 certification. Even using a pre-certified module, the end-product integration requires FCC approval or a Declaration of Conformity. This process takes 6-16 weeks, can require test lab time, and may require hardware modifications. It's entirely absent from the plan.

Caregiver push notification delivery (FCM/APNs) is treated as reliable for a life-safety alert path. FCM/APNs are best-effort, not guaranteed-delivery. The caregiver's phone may have notifications disabled, the app backgrounded/killed by iOS memory pressure, or the phone on DND/airplane mode. There is no SMS fallback SLA, no PSTN voice call fallback, and no multi-caregiver escalation chain specified. A single-point FCM failure means no alert.

OTA signing key management is unspecified. The ECDSA P-256 private key for firmware signing is critical — if compromised, an attacker can push arbitrary firmware to all deployed devices. There is no HSM requirement, no key ceremony procedure, no key rotation policy, and no revocation mechanism specified. This is both a security gap and a regulatory gap (cybersecurity documentation for FDA 510(k)).

Step 1 (risk management) and Step 3 (system architecture) have no dependency on each other and run in parallel. The hazard analysis outputs (risk controls, mitigations) must inform the architecture requirements. If Step 3 produces an architecture before Step 1 identifies a hazard that requires an architectural control (e.g., redundant alert paths for alert delivery failure), the architecture must be revised — causing cascading rework.

False positive rate specification (2%) applies only to 'normal ADL' bench simulation. No specification exists for sleep positions, bed egress, wheelchair use, exercise, or activities by users with mobility aids. The ADL dataset of 500 events is small and likely does not cover clinically relevant edge cases for an elderly population.
suggestions: Resolve software safety class (Class B vs C) as the first decision, before any other work begins. Document the hazard severity analysis that justifies the class. If any software function failure could contribute to a death, it is Class C. Get this decision signed off by your regulatory lead and an external notified body if targeting EU MDR simultaneously.

Run predicate device search in parallel with Step 1/2, not deferred to Step 18. Use FDA 510(k) database, product code MVO, and engage FDA pre-submission (Q-Sub) meeting early to confirm the regulatory pathway. Budget 3-6 months for FDA feedback cycles.

Add an IRB protocol submission step immediately before Step 22. Allow 8-12 weeks for IRB approval. Write the clinical protocol (including fall simulation methodology, inclusion/exclusion criteria, stopping rules, adverse event reporting) in Step 2 alongside the DHF structure.

Add a hybrid positioning fallback architecture: GPS (outdoor), WiFi positioning (indoor via Google/Apple location APIs), BLE beacon proximity (home environment option), with cell tower triangulation as last resort. Specify indoor vs outdoor accuracy requirements separately in the design inputs.

Commission a detailed power budget analysis as a blocking gate before PCB layout is finalized. Use nRF9161 Power Profiler Kit 2 measurements. If 400mAh cannot support 72h with representative duty cycle, increase to 600-800mAh or shrink the GPS duty cycle further — this decision must be made before Gerbers are fabricated.

Engage legal counsel specializing in telehealth/emergency services regulation before Step 11 (alert engine) is built. Obtain a RapidSOS partner agreement and confirm PSAP coverage in your target markets. Define false-alarm liability policy. Add a legal review gate before the 911 dispatch feature is enabled in production.

Increase clinical validation to minimum 100 participants for the pivotal study and engage a clinical research organization (CRO) with fall detection experience. Consider a wear-and-detect protocol with instrumented falls rather than supervised padded-mat falls to get more realistic biomechanical data.

Specify ML Core decision tree training dataset: source, size, demographic breakdown, independent validation holdout, and IRB approval for any human-derived data. Treat the decision tree as SOUP requiring qualification evidence per IEC 62304 §8.

Add Step 0: FCC pre-certification assessment and RF test planning. Determine whether to use a pre-certified nRF9161 module or do board-level FCC approval. Add FCC testing to the critical path — it must complete before commercial launch.
Add a Post-Market Surveillance plan and MDR/complaint handling SOP as deliverables in Step 18 or a new Step 18.5. These are required before commercial distribution.

Implement a multi-tier alert delivery chain: FCM push (primary, 0-5s) → SMS via Twilio (secondary, 5-15s) → PSTN voice call to caregiver (tertiary, 15-25s) → RapidSOS dispatch (30s). Each tier should fire if the previous tier is unacknowledged, not just the final escalation.

Specify ECDSA signing key management: HSM (AWS CloudHSM or similar) for key storage, documented key ceremony, dual-control key access, key rotation every 2 years, device revocation list mechanism. This is required for FDA cybersecurity documentation.

Make Step 3 depend on Step 1 (risk management outputs), or explicitly document that Step 3 is a preliminary architecture subject to revision after risk analysis completes. Track all architecture-impacting risk controls in the traceability matrix from Step 1 outputs.
missing: Post-Market Surveillance Plan (required by 21 CFR Part 822 and ISO 13485 §8.2.1)
Medical Device Reporting (MDR) and Complaint Handling SOP (21 CFR Part 803 / 820.198)
IRB protocol submission and approval process (21 CFR Part 50, 45 CFR Part 46)
FCC Part 15/22 RF certification step and timeline
Indoor positioning fallback strategy — GPS alone is insufficient for the intended use environment
OTA signing key management procedure (HSM, key ceremony, rotation, revocation)
Legal review of automatic 911 dispatch liability, FCC false-alarm rules, and state-by-state emergency dispatch laws
Multi-tier alert delivery with guaranteed fallback (SMS → voice call → 911) beyond single FCM push
De Novo pathway analysis as alternative to 510(k) if no adequate predicate is found
FDA Pre-Submission (Q-Sub) meeting step to confirm regulatory pathway before significant investment
COOP / multi-region AWS deployment strategy for the life-safety alert engine (single-region outage = no alerts)
Supplier qualification records and component obsolescence risk assessment for critical components (nRF9161, LSM6DSO32X, ATECC608B)
Cleaning and biocompatibility assessment per ISO 10993 — skin-contact wearable requires cytotoxicity and sensitization testing
Quality Management System (QMS) selection and setup (required for ISO 13485 certification and FDA 820.5)
ANVISA (Brazil), Health Canada, TGA (Australia) registration strategy if non-US markets are targeted
EU MDR 2017/745 Technical Documentation and Notified Body engagement if CE marking is required
Software anomaly resolution procedure (IEC 62304 §6.2.5) — defects found during V&V must go through a documented resolution process
security_risks: OTA signing key stored without HSM: if the ECDSA private key is compromised (developer laptop, CI/CD secret leak), an attacker can push malicious firmware to the entire deployed device fleet — silently disabling fall detection or enabling remote surveillance. No revocation mechanism is specified.

RapidSOS API endpoint is a high-value target: an attacker who can forge a fall event (exploiting a confidence score bypass or MQTT topic ACL gap) can trigger automatic 911 dispatch to any address, causing emergency resource exhaustion. Rate limiting on the fall-event endpoint is specified but its enforcement under a spoofed device identity is not verified.

mTLS device certificates provisioned at manufacturing (ATECC608B) have no revocation mechanism specified. If a device is stolen or its certificate extracted, it can permanently impersonate a legitimate device. No OCSP/CRL infrastructure is defined.

The offline flash buffer (8KB, Step 10) stores unprocessed fall events. If the buffer contents are not encrypted at rest on the nRF9161 flash, an attacker with physical access (JTAG/SWD) can read buffered GPS positions and health events — PHI exposure without decryption.

The 30-second no-response escalation window is a denial-of-service vector: an attacker who can flood the alert engine with high-confidence false fall events (by compromising one device's credentials) can saturate Celery workers, delay legitimate escalations, and potentially deplete the RapidSOS API quota causing real emergencies to go undispatched.

The caregiver app uses OAuth 2.0 + PKCE via Auth0, but biometric unlock on subsequent opens does not re-authenticate to the server — it only unlocks the local token store. A stolen unlocked phone gives permanent caregiver access with no session expiry on the device. Server-side session binding to device fingerprint is not specified.

IEC 62443 is cited as a security standard (Step 4) but it governs industrial control systems, not medical device cybersecurity. The correct references are FDA 2023 Cybersecurity Guidance, IEC 81001-5-1:2021, and NIST SP 800-213 (already cited). Relying on IEC 62443 framing may produce a security architecture that passes an industrial audit but fails FDA cybersecurity review.

GPS position data transmitted at 1Hz during emergencies over MQTT QoS 1 means precise real-time location of a vulnerable elderly person is continuously accessible to anyone who can read the MQTT broker stream. MQTT topic ACLs are mentioned but the broker-level authorization model (per-device topic isolation) and the cloud-side storage encryption for live location data are not fully specified.
final_review.summary: This plan is unusually comprehensive for a medtech IoT system — the technology choices are sensible (nRF9161, LSM6DSO32X ML Core, Zephyr RTOS, TimescaleDB, RapidSOS), the regulatory structure is well-intentioned, and the testing depth is above average for an early-stage plan. However, it cannot score above 54 for an FDA Class II submission target because it contains multiple blocking issues that will cause either FDA rejection or post-market regulatory action: the IEC 62304 software safety class is almost certainly wrong and must be resolved before any V&V work begins; the 510(k) predicate strategy is unvalidated and deferred 17 steps too late; no IRB approval step exists for human subject studies; indoor GPS is the primary use case failure mode with no mitigation; 400mAh supporting 72h with active LTE-M and GPS is a credible but unproven claim that must be budgeted before PCB fabrication; automatic 911 dispatch has zero legal review; and post-market surveillance and MDR complaint handling procedures — both non-negotiable FDA requirements — are entirely absent. The security architecture is solid in concept but has critical gaps in key management and revocation infrastructure. To reach production readiness for a regulated submission, the plan needs a regulatory strategy gate (Step 0: predicate + pathway + Q-Sub), a reclassification decision on IEC 62304 safety class, IRB process, indoor positioning fallback, FCC certification track, post-market procedures, and legal review of the 911 dispatch feature — before committing to the current hardware BOM and firmware architecture.

flaws: IEC 62304 Software Safety Class misclassification: Class B ('injury possible') is almost certainly wrong. An undetected fall causing an elderly person to lie unresponsive for hours can be fatal — that is Class C ('death possible'). Class B requires only software unit testing; Class C requires full integration and system testing with traceability to every software item. If FDA reviewers disagree with your Class B justification, the entire IEC 62304 software documentation package must be redone. This is submission-killing if wrong.

510(k) predicate assumption is unvalidated and deferred too late. The predicate device search is listed as an acceptance criterion in Step 18 — after 17 steps of design work. If no adequate predicate exists under MVO, or if FDA classifies automatic emergency dispatch as a novel feature requiring De Novo, the entire regulatory strategy collapses. Predicate identification must happen in Step 2, not Step 18.

No IRB/Ethics Board approval step. Step 22 involves controlled fall simulations with elderly participants on padded mats and a 30-day field trial. This requires IRB approval (45 CFR Part 46 / 21 CFR Part 50) before any human subject study begins. Proceeding without IRB approval invalidates the clinical data for FDA submission and exposes the organization to serious legal liability.

The '≤10 meters' GPS spec will fail the majority of real-world use cases.

Step 6 acceptance criteria require a power budget analysis, but the 400mAh figure is already specified as a fixed constraint in the BOM — if the power budget fails, the hardware must be redesigned. This should be validated before committing to the form factor.

False-positive 911 calls can result in liability under FCC rules and state statutes. No legal review step exists in this plan. RapidSOS NG911 API access requires a signed agreement with RapidSOS, carrier integration agreements, and may not have PSAP coverage in all geographies. The plan treats this as a pure engineering problem.

Clinical validation sample size is inadequate for FDA evidence. 30 participants (10 for field trial) is far below what FDA expects for a Class II device with a primary safety endpoint. Predicate devices in the fall detection space have used 100-300 participants for pivotal validation. The 'controlled fall simulation on padded mats' does not replicate real fall biomechanics. FDA will likely require clinical data from spontaneous or minimally-assisted falls. This validation plan will not support a 510(k) submission.

MDR (Medical Device Reporting) procedures under 21 CFR Part 803 for adverse event reporting are completely absent. These are not optional and must exist before commercialization.

Even using a pre-certified module, the end-product integration requires FCC approval or a Declaration of Conformity. This process takes 6-16 weeks, can require test lab time, and may require hardware modifications. It's entirely absent from the plan.

history.summary: This plan is unusually comprehensive for a medtech IoT system — the technology choices are sensible (nRF9161, LSM6DSO32X ML Core, Zephyr RTOS, TimescaleDB, RapidSOS), the regulatory structure is well-intentioned, and the testing depth is above average for an early-stage plan. However, it cannot score above 54 for an FDA Class II submission target because it contains multiple blocking issues that will cause either FDA rejection or post-market regulatory action: the IEC 62304 software safety class is almost certainly wrong and must be resolved before any V&V work begins; the 510(k) predicate strategy is unvalidated and deferred 17 steps too late; no IRB approval step exists for human subject studies; indoor GPS is the primary use case failure mode with no mitigation; 400mAh supporting 72h with active LTE-M and GPS is a credible but unproven claim that must be budgeted before PCB fabrication; automatic 911 dispatch has zero legal review; and post-market surveillance and MDR complaint handling procedures — both non-negotiable FDA requirements — are entirely absent. The security architecture is solid in concept but has critical gaps in key management and revocation infrastructure. To reach production readiness for a regulated submission, the plan needs a regulatory strategy gate (Step 0: predicate + pathway + Q-Sub), a reclassification decision on IEC 62304 safety class, IRB process, indoor positioning fallback, FCC certification track, post-market procedures, and legal review of the 911 dispatch feature — before committing to the current hardware BOM and firmware architecture.
